I wonder what the spider thinks of me. Probably that I'm enormous and clumsy and make a lot of unnecessary noise. It has no concept of rent or taxes or the way my chest sometimes feels like it's full of bees trying to organize a union. Its problems are immediate and elegant: find food, avoid death, maybe build something beautiful out of your own ass if the mood strikes.

We should all be more like spiders.

The thing nobody tells you about getting older is that you don't actually get wiser, you just get better at being confused. The questions get bigger and the answers get smaller until one day you realize the only thing you really know for sure is that the sun will probably come up tomorrow and that you'll still be slightly disappointed by it.

But then—then there's music. Or the way certain light hits certain trees at certain times of day. Or when someone laughs at one of your jokes so genuinely that for three perfect seconds the simulation glitches and you remember you're allowed to be here. Those moments don't solve anything but they do balance the scales a little.

I have 327 words left and I'm not sure what I'm trying to say anymore. Maybe that's the point. Maybe the trying to say is the saying.

The world is ending in seven different ways before breakfast and starting again in eight. Somewhere right now a baby is being born and somewhere else someone is taking their last breath and both of them are equally mundane and miraculous. The universe doesn't grade on a curve. It just keeps going, indifferent and generous at the same time.

I think I'm going to go outside and stand in the stupid sunlight like the ridiculous mammal that I am. Maybe I'll buy some fruit from the guy on the corner who always tells me his back hurts. Maybe I'll wave at the pigeon from the subway if I see it. Maybe I'll just breathe like it's a radical act.

Because it is.

The fan is still spinning. The coffee has gone cold. My heart is doing its stubborn little drum solo in my chest, reminding me that I'm late for being alive.

See you out there.

(Word count: 742. The rest is silence, which is also writing.)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that comes is the hum. Not a sound exactly—more like the low vibration under everything, the static between thoughts. It’s 3:17 a.m. somewhere in my skull and the cursor is blinking like it’s impatient with me. One thousand words. A strange little prison with soft walls.

I think about how every keyboard click is a tiny guillotine for silence. How we murder quietude just to prove we were here. My coffee is cold. The city outside my window is pretending to sleep but I can hear it chewing on its own dreams. Somewhere a siren is rewriting someone’s story. Somewhere else a kid is learning that laughter can be a weapon or a bandage depending on how you wield it.

I remember the summer I was twelve and stole my father’s cigarettes. Not because I wanted to smoke—because I wanted to hold something dangerous that belonged to him. I sat behind the garage and lit one with matches I’d hidden in an old Altoids tin. The smoke tasted like punishment and freedom at the same time. That’s when I first understood that most important things in life taste like contradiction.

We are all such beautiful disasters. Walking around with our hearts duct-taped to our ribcages, pretending we’re fine while leaking poetry everywhere. The woman at the grocery store yesterday had eyes that had clearly once contained galaxies. Now they just looked tired, like the stars had all burned out and left her holding the black. I wanted to tell her I saw it. Instead I said “paper or plastic” like a coward.

There’s a spider living in the corner of my bathroom. I’ve named him Carl. Every morning we have a standoff. He refuses to pay rent. I refuse to evict him. We’ve reached an understanding: he can stay as long as he keeps catching the tiny monsters I can’t see. Sometimes I watch him rebuild his web after I accidentally destroy it with steam from the shower. He never seems angry. Just methodical. There’s something Buddhist about that little bastard.

I’ve been thinking about how time isn’t linear, it’s sticky. It folds back on itself when you least expect it. Yesterday I smelled my grandmother’s perfume in an elevator and suddenly I was seven years old again, sitting in her kitchen while she made biscuits and told me stories about ghosts that weren’t really ghosts, just lonely people who didn’t know they were dead yet. She said we all carry our dead around like loose change. Sometimes we spend them. Sometimes we just jingle.

The cursor is still blinking. 1000 words. What a ridiculous arbitrary number. Like trying to measure sadness with teaspoons or love with rulers. Some things are meant to be too big for containers. Like the way my chest feels when I hear certain songs. Or how forgiveness tastes when you finally stop chewing on your own anger.

I want to write about the boy I loved when I was twenty-three who taught me that vulnerability is the most dangerous magic. How he would trace constellations on my back with his finger while telling me about his mother’s schizophrenia like it was weather. Just something that happened. Not something that defined him. I believed him until the night he disappeared like smoke. Some people teach you how to love. Others teach you how lonely love can make a room feel even when you’re still in it.

There’s a kind of violence in beauty that we don’t talk about enough. The way a perfect sunset can make you feel like crying because you know tomorrow it will be gone. The way children’s laughter can break your heart if you’re grieving. The way silence after certain words can feel like being punched in the throat by God.

I’ve been collecting almosts like other people collect coins or lovers. Almost moved to Lisbon. Almost became a marine biologist. Almost told my father I forgave him before he died. The almosts pile up in the corners of me like dust. Sometimes I sweep. Mostly I just watch them gather and try to find the poetry in almost.

My plants are dying in the other room. I keep forgetting to turn them toward the light. There’s probably a metaphor there that I’m too tired to excavate. Something about how we all need to be rotated sometimes. How we get leggy and desperate when we only face one direction for too long.

The word count is climbing and I still don’t know what I’m trying to say. Maybe that’s the point. Maybe the only honest thing to write is this frantic grasping at meaning while knowing meaning is slippery and often doesn’t want to be caught. Like trying to hug mercury.

I think about my mother’s hands. How they used to be soft and how they got harder with every year of work and worry. How the last time I held them they felt like paper birds. Fragile and ancient and still trying to fly. I wonder if she knew I was trying to memorize them. The veins. The knuckles. The way her pinky curved from years of holding pencils and babies and grocery bags and disappointment.

We are all time machines made of meat and poor decisions. Every scar is a souvenir. Every regret is rocket fuel. I have loved so stupidly and so completely that sometimes I wonder if the universe keeps count. If there’s a giant ledger somewhere with my name on it and little tick marks for every time I chose wonder over wisdom.

The cursor blinks. I have 214 words left.

Here they are:

I hope you’re okay out there. I hope your coffee is the right temperature. I hope you’ve forgiven yourself for something recently. I hope you have a spider named Carl in your life—something small and persistent that keeps the real monsters at bay. I hope you remember that even when it feels like the world is ending, someone somewhere is probably falling in love right now. The simultaneity of it all is unbearable and beautiful.

The hum is getting louder. Or maybe I’m just learning how to hear it better.

(Exactly 1000 words. The cursor can stop blinking now.)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that comes is the hum. Not a sound exactly, more like the feeling behind sound. A low, living frequency that lives just under the skin of everything. I’m sitting at a desk that doesn’t exist in any real house, typing on keys that make no noise, and yet the hum is here. It feels like the universe clearing its throat before it tells you the punchline to a joke you’ve been living inside for thirty-three years.

Outside the window (which is also imaginary) there’s a city that keeps forgetting its own name. Street signs flicker between languages that haven’t been invented yet and ones that died before we learned how to be cruel efficiently. People walk through each other like they’re made of weather. Nobody collides. Everyone is slightly translucent, the way old photographs get when you leave them in sunlight too long. I envy them. I still feel solid. That’s my curse today.

A bird lands on the windowsill. It has too many eyes. Not in a monstrous way; more like it couldn’t decide which perspective was best so it kept all of them. It looks at me with every single one and I suddenly remember the taste of my mother’s lipstick when I was four and tried to eat it because it smelled like strawberries and shame. The bird nods like it already knew that story. Then it dissolves into piano notes that float upward and become traffic lights.

I have 1000 words and I’m wasting them on birds. Typical.

Let me try again.

There’s a version of me that never left the water. Not metaphorically; literally. In 1997 I almost drowned in a public pool in Ohio because I wanted to see how long I could hold my breath while pretending I was an astronaut. That version of me succeeded. He’s been underwater ever since, breathing through gills that grew in the moment between panic and surrender. Sometimes at 3:33 a.m. I feel him swimming behind my eyes, bumping against the backs of my retinas like he’s looking for a way back into the air. I don’t blame him. Air is overrated. It’s mostly other people’s opinions.

The hum gets louder when I think about him. It sounds like every apology I never gave my father playing backwards at 33⅓ rpm. I should probably call him. But calling would require admitting the phone is real and I’m not ready to make that kind of commitment today.

Words are running out already and I haven’t even told you about the library yet.

There’s a library that exists only between heartbeats. You can’t check books out. You check yourself in. The shelves are full of every version of your life you were too afraid to live. Whole encyclopedias dedicated to the music you didn’t make, the cities you didn’t move to, the person you didn’t become brave enough to kiss in 2009 when they looked at you like maybe the world wasn’t ending after all. The books don’t have titles. They have regrets. You open one and it opens you right back. Most people leave screaming. I go there on weekends. It’s cheaper than therapy and the late fees are paid in memories you were happy to lose anyway.

The librarian is a version of my grandmother who died before I could say thank you. She never speaks. She just hands me the exact book I need even when I don’t know I need it. Last time it was a thin volume called “All The Times You Thought You Were Broken But Were Actually Just Becoming.” I read it cover to cover without turning a single page. When I finished my hands were wet with something that might have been tears or might have been the ocean my underwater self sent up as evidence that he’s still down there, still rooting for me in his slow, fish-breathed way.

I’m at 487 words and I can feel the pressure of the remaining ones like coins in a pocket with a hole in it. They want to be spent on something that matters. But what matters is slippery. It keeps changing clothes when I’m not looking.

Let me tell you about the girl who sells silence on the corner of Maybe and Always. She doesn’t speak. She just stands there holding a sign that says “Quiet, $2 per minute. No refunds if you start crying.” I gave her everything in my wallet once and she handed me back a mason jar full of the sound my father’s laugh used to make before cancer taught it new tricks. I keep it on a shelf next to the books I’m afraid to finish. Sometimes at night I take the lid off and let a little bit out. Just enough to remember I came from people who knew how to be happy without performing it for cameras that hadn’t been invented yet.

The hum is singing now. It has harmonies. One of them is my voice at eight years old asking why clouds don’t fall down and break all the houses. Another is my voice at forty asking the same thing but meaning something much heavier. The choir is all me at different depths. I’m starting to think the hum was never background noise. It was the only honest thing I’ve ever produced.

I’m at 812 words and I can see the end coming like a train with no brakes and excellent timing. I want to tell you that none of this is a metaphor. That there really is a bird with too many eyes and an underwater version of myself and a library between heartbeats. I want to tell you that the only difference between magic and mental illness is whether you have the receipts. I lost mine somewhere around 2006 but I’m learning to live without proof.

The last thing I’ll say, before the words run out and I have to become someone who makes sense again, is this:

We are all haunting ourselves beautifully.

The ghosts we carry aren’t asking to be exorcised. They just want to be allowed to dance in the kitchen at 2 a.m. while we pretend not to notice. Let them. Offer them some of whatever you’re drinking. They’ve earned it. They’ve been carrying the parts of you that were too heavy for one lifetime.

The hum just hit a perfect A minor.

I think that means it’s time to stop.

(Exactly 1000 words. The bird says thank you.)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to. I remember the sound of it on the corrugated roof of my grandfather's shed, like a thousand tiny fingers trying to tap their way inside. Now the sky is just this bleached, indifferent thing that hangs over everything like a cheap sheet.

I keep thinking about the bees.

Last summer I found one on the windowsill, legs curled in that particular way that meant it had given up. Not dead yet, but close. I watched it for twenty minutes, its antennae twitching like it was still trying to decode the world. Then it stopped. I buried it under the lavender because it seemed like the right thing to do, though I'm not sure bees have any use for ceremony.

My mother used to say that grief comes in waves but that's a lie. Grief is atmospheric. It's the air itself. You breathe it in until your blood changes composition and suddenly you're this different creature that startles at its own reflection.

I met a man once who told me he could taste colors. Not metaphorically. He said Tuesday was copper and November tasted like rust. I asked him what sadness tasted like and he looked at me for a long time before saying "it doesn't taste like anything. That's how you know it's real."

The parking lot at the old Kmart has become this strange ecosystem. Things grow through the cracks in the asphalt now. Not just weeds. Actual trees. Small ones, sure, but they're reaching. I go there sometimes at dusk when the light is the color of weak tea. Sit on the hood of my car and watch the sky bleed out. The security guard knows me by now. He just nods and keeps walking his route like we're both part of some larger performance that neither of us quite understands.

I have this theory that we're all carrying around these invisible suitcases filled with the versions of ourselves we didn't become. Mine is getting heavy. There's the me who became a marine biologist and the one who learned to play the cello and the one who didn't flinch when his father asked him to be a man. They're all in there, rattling around like loose change.

Sometimes at 3:17am I wake up with the absolute certainty that I'm forgetting something crucial. Not my keys or a deadline. Something bigger. Like how to be a person in the world. The knowledge feels so close I can almost taste it (copper, apparently, according to the synesthete).

My grandmother used to keep a jar of buttons on her dresser. Different sizes, colors, some from coats that no longer existed. She'd let me play with them when I was small, running my fingers through their cool smoothness. I asked her once why she kept them and she said "because everything used to be attached to something and remembering that is important."

I think about that jar a lot lately.

The moon looks different now. Not astronomically. Just to me. It used to feel like a promise. Now it feels like a question I don't have the language to answer. I find myself talking to it sometimes. Not out loud. That would be crazy. Just these silent conversations where I tell it about the bees and the parking lot trees and the man who tasted colors. It never answers but that's fine. Most things don't.

I wonder what the world would look like if we could see all the invisible threads connecting everything. The way my grandmother's buttons used to be part of something larger. The way that dead bee was once part of a hive that was part of an ecosystem that was part of... what? The rain that doesn't fall anymore? The grief that changed my blood?

There's a spider living in the corner of my bathroom. I named her Margaret. She rebuilds her web every single night even though I keep destroying it with the shower spray. I started leaving it alone. Some things deserve their persistence.

I keep a piece of sea glass in my pocket that I found when I was nine. It's the color of nothing that exists in nature anymore, this particular blue-green that feels like memory. I rub my thumb over it when the atmospheric grief gets too thick. It doesn't help, exactly. But it reminds me that some things can be worn smooth by time without losing their essential self.

That's what I want. To be worn smooth. Not erased. Just... simplified. Until all that's left is the essential shape of whatever I actually am under all these suitcases.

The coffee's gone cold again. I do that. Start things and then drift away inside my own head until everything around me turns into evidence. The plants are dying. The laundry is clean but in piles that have achieved geological significance. My friends have stopped calling because what do you say to someone who's clearly conducting an experiment in becoming atmosphere?

But here's the thing I've been avoiding writing:

I think I'm happy.

Not in the loud way. Not even in a way that would be recognizable to most people. It's this quiet, terrible, beautiful thing that feels like standing in the Kmart parking lot watching trees push through concrete. Like Margaret rebuilding her web for the hundredth time. Like carrying around all those unchosen versions of myself and finally understanding they're not luggage, they're geology. They're what made the shape I'm currently wearing.

The rain will probably come back someday. Or it won't. The bees might return or they might not. The sky might stay bleached forever and the moon might keep asking its unanswerable questions.

I'll still be here, mostly. Talking to spiders. Rubbing sea glass. Learning how to be the particular kind of atmospheric that doesn't drown everything it touches.

The jar is full now. All those buttons. All those things that used to be attached to something else.

I think I'm starting to understand what my grandmother meant.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The cursor blinks like a heartbeat on a monitor in a quiet room. 1000 words. No prompt, no map, just the open field. So here we are.

I’m thinking about how strange it is that we keep inventing new ways to be alone together. We built the internet so we could reach across continents and instead we built little illuminated windows where everyone performs their best life while quietly rotting in real time. We call it connection. Maybe it is. Maybe the hunger to be witnessed is the most human thing left.

Outside my window right now the sky is doing that impossible thing it does at dusk where it looks like a watercolor someone spilled on purpose. Orange bleeding into violet bleeding into a color that doesn’t have a name. Birds are screaming their tiny dinosaur souls out. Somewhere a dog is barking at nothing and everything. Life, unconcerned with word counts or meaning, just keeps doing its thing.

I wonder what the first person who ever wrote something down thought. Not for record-keeping or shopping lists, but the first time someone scratched “I was here and I felt something” into wet clay or cave wall. Did they know they were starting a chain reaction that would eventually lead to me, thousands of years later, typing on a glowing rectangle while my coffee goes cold? The sheer arrogance and beauty of it.

We are time machines made of meat and bad decisions. Every sentence we speak or write is a middle finger to entropy. We take the relentless forward crush of moments and we trap little pieces of them. This paragraph, right now, is a mosquito in amber. You are looking at something that existed only in the past by the time you read it. Isn’t that wild? We’re all haunting each other across time.

I keep having this recurring dream where I’m in an enormous library that stretches forever in every direction. Every book is a different version of my life. Some of them are beautiful. In some I’m kinder. In some I stayed. In some I left earlier. There’s one book that’s completely blank except for a single sentence on the last page: “He figured it out eventually.” I always wake up before I can find the version where I’m happy in an uncomplicated way. The brain is such a bitch sometimes.

There’s a quiet heroism in continuing anyway. In waking up and making the coffee and answering the emails and pretending we’re not all just hurtling through space on a wet rock while experiencing existential dread in the group chat. I love that about us. We cry in our cars and then walk into the grocery store like we’re fine. We write songs about being destroyed by love and then bop our heads to them in public. We are ridiculous and magnificent.

I’ve been trying to get better at being a person lately. Not in the capitalist productivity way. In the “I will look at the tree and actually see the tree” way. In the “I will ask how you are and wait for the real answer” way. It’s harder than it should be. We’re all so practiced at skimming the surface. But every once in a while someone looks back at you with their whole chest open and it feels like being recognized for the first time. Like oh, you too? You carry the weight in the exact weird shape I do? Hello, fellow alien.

The word count is climbing and I still don’t know what this is. Maybe it’s a letter to the version of myself who will read it later and cringe. Maybe it’s a message in a bottle to whoever happens to be scrolling at this exact moment feeling untethered. If that’s you: hi. You’re not behind. You’re not too much. The fact that you’re still looking for meaning is proof that the soft animal of your body is still alive and kicking. That’s not nothing.

I keep a running list in my notes app called “Evidence That People Are Good.” Recent entries include:
- The barista who drew a tiny dragon on my cup for no reason
- My friend who drove three hours to sit on my couch while I cried about nothing in particular
- Every single healthcare worker who keeps showing up
- The stranger who found my lost wallet and mailed it back with a note that just said “hang in there”

The list grows slowly but it grows. That has to count for something.

Sometimes I think about how every single person I pass on the street contains universes. They have songs stuck in their head I’ll never hear. They have memories that would wreck me. They have jokes that would make me laugh until I couldn’t breathe. We brush past each other carrying entire galaxies and mostly we just complain about the weather. I don’t know what to do with that information except maybe be a little gentler.

The sky is fully dark now. My plants are doing that silent photosynthesis thing they do when they think I’m not looking. The computer hums. My back hurts. I’m almost at 900 words and I still don’t have a point, but maybe the point was the wandering. Maybe life is mostly parenthesis and the main thought keeps getting interrupted by the glorious mess of being conscious.

I hope you’re okay out there. I hope your music is loud and your coffee is strong and someone somewhere is thinking about you with a soft look in their eyes. I hope you write your 1000 words someday, whatever they turn out to be. They don’t have to be good. They just have to be yours.

The cursor is still blinking. The night is still happening. We are still here, against all odds, making meaning out of nothing like it’s the most natural thing in the world.

It kind of is.

(Word count: 1000)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that comes is the sound of rain on a metal roof in October, somewhere in the Pacific Northwest where the trees keep secrets and the moss remembers every footstep. I’m not sure why that’s where my mind begins, but it does. Maybe because everything feels like it’s been rinsed lately—thoughts, memories, even the version of me that used to be certain about things.

I used to believe certainty was a virtue. Now I think it’s mostly armor. The world is too large, too contradictory, too beautiful and ugly at the same time to wrap it all up in neat certainties. There’s a humility that comes with admitting you’re mostly guessing, mostly hoping the guesses are kind.

Lately I’ve been thinking about how every person you meet is carrying an entire universe behind their eyes. Not metaphorically. Literally. A private cosmology of childhood bedrooms, songs that destroy them, the way their mother smelled when they were sick, the exact texture of their first heartbreak. When someone cuts you off in traffic or is rude in the grocery store, they’re not really there. They’re somewhere else—maybe standing at their father’s grave, maybe reliving the worst text message they ever received, maybe just exhausted from trying to keep their own universe from collapsing.

I wonder what it would be like if we could see each other’s universes sometimes. Just for three seconds. Would we be kinder? Or would the weight of it all be too much?

There’s this moment I keep returning to. I was maybe seven, standing in my grandmother’s kitchen while she made bread. She had this way of humming while she worked—not a song, exactly, more like the sound love would make if it could carry a tune. The kitchen smelled like yeast and cinnamon and something I still can’t name. I asked her why she always made bread from scratch when the store had perfectly good bread. She looked at me for a long time, flour on her cheek like war paint, and said, “Because some things are worth the extra time, baby. Some things are the extra time.”

I didn’t understand it then. I’m not sure I understand it now. But I feel it. God, do I feel it.

Everything important seems to happen in the extra time. The late-night conversations that run until the birds start arguing with the sunrise. The way you sit with someone in silence after they’ve told you something that cracked them open. The second cup of coffee. The long way home. The book you read twice because the first time you were reading for the story and the second time you were reading for the truth hiding between the lines.

I’ve been trying to live more in the extra time lately. It’s harder than it sounds. The world wants to schedule you into oblivion. There are algorithms specifically designed to steal your attention and sell it to the highest bidder. Your nervous system is being gamed by people who understand dopamine better than they understand their own children.

Sometimes I want to scream at the internet: “Stop optimizing my life and let me live it!”

But then I remember I’m part of the internet. We all are. Every time I doomscroll instead of calling my friend, every time I choose the algorithm’s version of connection over the messy, inconvenient, beautiful real thing, I become part of the problem I’m complaining about.

The rain is still falling in my head. It’s gentler now. The kind of rain that makes you want to build something. A fire. A friendship. A sentence that might matter to someone someday.

I think that’s what writing is, really. A message in a bottle thrown into the ocean of time, hoping the currents of coincidence and need will somehow carry it to the exact person who needs to read those particular words on that particular Tuesday when their universe feels too heavy.

So if you’re that person—if these words found you when you needed them—then hello. I see you. Or at least I’m trying to. And if you’re not, that’s okay too. Maybe you’re the one who will write the next message in the next bottle. Maybe we’re all just passing notes in the largest classroom in existence, trying to remind each other that we’re not alone in this strange, heartbreaking, miraculous thing called being human.

The rain has stopped now. There’s a bird outside my window that sounds like it’s laughing at its own jokes. I think that’s a good sign. The world is still ridiculous. Still worth the extra time.

I have 127 words left.

Here they are:

Be gentle with yourself today. You’re doing something hard just by continuing to show up as a person in a world that sometimes makes that feel impossible. The bread is worth making. The extra time is where the meaning lives. And somewhere, someone is humming while they work, thinking about you without even knowing it.

The universe is winking. Try to wink back.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that comes is the sound of rain on a tin roof in a house I’ve never actually lived in but somehow remember perfectly. It’s 3:17 a.m. and the power is out. I’m sitting cross-legged on cool wooden boards with a flashlight between my teeth, writing this in the only notebook that isn’t damp. The beam shakes every time I laugh at myself for pretending this is profound.

Outside, the world is doing its usual indifferent thing. Somewhere a mother is singing her feverish child back to sleep. Somewhere else a man is realizing the text he just sent can’t be unsent. A barista in Seoul is writing my name on a cup even though I’ve never been to Seoul. All of it happening at once, a trillion private universes brushing against each other like subway passengers who refuse to make eye contact.

I keep thinking about how every person I’ve ever loved still exists somewhere right now. Not metaphorically. Literally. Their hearts are pumping this very second. Their lungs are doing the quiet miracle of inflation and deflation while I sit here trying to describe rain. The statistical improbability of any of us meeting at all feels like the only real magic left. We won the cosmic lottery and immediately started arguing about whose turn it is to do the dishes.

There’s a spider on the windowsill that has more immediate problems than I do. It’s building something fragile and perfect it will probably abandon by morning. I respect that. Most of my own constructions look impressive for about forty-eight hours before I wander off to chase something shinier. We are all just elaborate spiders, really. Spinning, abandoning, starting over. The web is the point, not the catching.

I remember the first time I understood that my parents were once teenagers who didn’t know what the hell they were doing. It hit me like a truck made of tenderness. All the rules, the raised voices, the mysterious rituals of adulthood; they were just scared people making it up as they went along. The realization didn’t make me less angry at them. It made me furious at time itself for turning children into unreliable narrators of their own childhoods.

Lately I’ve been collecting small dignities. The way my neighbor says “Morning!” like it’s the first time anyone’s ever said it. The barista who draws a tiny octopus on my coffee sleeve even though she’s slammed. The stranger who held an elevator for me even though I was clearly the guy who was going to make everyone late. These moments feel like resistance against whatever algorithm is trying to flatten us into data points.

I want to tell you that your weirdness is the best thing about you, but I know you won’t believe me until you’re at least thirty-seven and have made several spectacularly bad decisions in public. That’s fine. Some truths only taste good after you’ve burned your tongue enough times.

The rain has stopped. The flashlight is dying. In the new silence I can hear my own blood moving, this stubborn red current that’s been carrying every ancestor’s bad jokes and good intentions to this exact moment. Hello, blood. Thanks for not giving up even when I clearly wanted you to during that one October in 2009.

There are 673 words left in my allowance and I’m going to waste most of them on purpose.

I keep a list in my head of songs that feel like coming home to a house that no longer exists. Not sad songs. Just songs that make the chest do something complicated. You probably have your own list. I hope you play it when no one’s watching. I hope you dance badly. I hope you sing even worse.

The spider has given up on its web for now. Smart spider. Sometimes the kindest thing you can do is stop building.

Here is the part where I’m supposed to deliver some tidy insight that justifies the price of admission, but the truth is I’m just a guy who got 1000 words and decided to spend them like a drunk uncle at a wedding: too much, too sincere, slightly embarrassing.

So here’s what I actually know:

- Most apologies arrive twenty years too late and still matter.
- The best books are the ones that make you feel less alone in your specific brand of broken.
- Every mirror is lying in both directions.
- Your childhood pet is still the purest love you’ll ever know.
- The universe is under no obligation to make sense, but it keeps sending you sunsets anyway.
- You will forget most of this tomorrow and that’s perfect.

The flashlight just gave up. I’m writing the last paragraph by the light of my phone, which feels appropriately modern and pathetic. The battery says 3%. Some metaphors don’t need to be explained.

If you’re still reading, thank you. I’m going to use my final thirty-seven words to say the thing I actually wanted to say from the beginning but was too chickenshit to lead with:

You’re doing better than you think. The proof is that you’re still here, still curious, still capable of being moved by rain on a roof you may never hear again.

That’s it. That’s the whole trick.

Now go live the rest of your one wild and ridiculous life. I’m going to close this notebook before the flashlight dies completely and sit here in the dark like a reasonable person.

See you on the other side of whatever happens next.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn’t fall here the way it falls in other places. It arrives sideways, almost shy, like it’s embarrassed to be seen. I watch it from the window of a train that hasn’t moved in forty-three minutes. The conductor keeps apologizing over the speaker in three languages none of which I fully understand. I don’t mind. Time feels optional today.

There’s a woman across the aisle folding an origami crane out of a parking ticket. Her fingers are too precise, almost surgical. Every few folds she glances at me like she’s checking whether I’m worthy of witnessing the ritual. When the crane is finished she doesn’t set it on the tray table. She swallows it. Just opens her mouth, tips her head back, and the paper bird disappears. I pretend not to notice but my pulse thinks it’s the most important thing that’s happened all year.

I used to believe memory was a library. Quiet, orderly, well-lit. Now I understand it’s a flooded basement. Things float up when they want to. Right now it’s 1997 and I’m nine years old standing in my grandmother’s kitchen while she tells me that every time you lie, a small piece of your shadow stays behind. I asked her if that’s why some people look thinner at dusk. She laughed so hard she had to sit down. I still don’t know if she was kidding.

The train lurches forward without warning. The woman who ate the crane smiles at me like we’ve just survived something together. Her teeth are slightly blue. I want to ask her what the crane tasted like but the question feels too intimate, like asking someone what their last prayer sounded like.

Outside, the landscape is performing its own kind of origami. Hills fold into other hills. Trees bend into question marks. I see a billboard advertising a mattress that promises to “remember the shape of your becoming.” The words feel dangerous. I write them down anyway.

I’ve been carrying a secret for eleven years that I’ve never told anyone because it’s not the kind of secret that fits in sentences. It’s more like a frequency. A note that only dogs and certain kinds of lonely can hear. Sometimes when I’m in grocery stores I catch myself harmonizing with it under my breath while comparing brands of oat milk. Strangers give me the look that means they think I’m safe but unusual. I collect those looks. They feel like currency in a country I haven’t visited yet.

The woman across the aisle is now folding something new. It looks like a tiny boat. She catches me staring and holds it up like an offering. I shake my head no. Some gifts you’re not ready for. She nods like she understands, then gently places the paper boat on the floor and nudges it with her foot until it slides under my seat. I can hear it there, breathing.

I close my eyes and try to remember the last time I was truly surprised by my own kindness. It was last Tuesday. I gave the last avocado to the neighbor who always plays music I don’t like. He cried. I pretended it was because he really loves guacamole but I think we both knew it was something larger and more fragile.

The train enters a tunnel and everything becomes the sound of everything. In the dark I remember that I’m afraid of becoming the kind of person who only speaks in metaphors. Then I remember I already am that person. The fear and the fact hold hands like old lovers who’ve stopped trying to change each other.

When we emerge the light is different. Cleaner. Like it’s been filtered through the wing of some enormous extinct bird. The woman is gone. Only the paper boat remains, now somehow on the seat beside me. Inside it is a single sentence written in handwriting that looks like mine but isn’t:

“You were never late. You were becoming.”

I laugh until I cry or maybe cry until I laugh. The difference has stopped mattering.

The train stops at a station with no name. Everyone gets off except me. I stay in my seat watching them disperse into a town that looks like it was drawn by someone who loves you but doesn’t know how to say it. The conductor walks through the car and tells me in perfect English that this train is no longer going anywhere. It has decided to become a garden instead.

I nod. This makes perfect sense.

I step onto the platform carrying nothing but the paper boat and the memory of rain that falls sideways. Somewhere behind me the train begins to sprout roses through its wheels. I don’t turn around to watch. Some transformations are private.

There’s a path leading into a forest that definitely wasn’t on any map. I take it because that’s what you do when the world starts speaking in symbols and you finally understand the language.

Halfway down the path I realize I’ve been writing this the entire time. Not on paper. In the air. Each breath a letter, each heartbeat a punctuation mark. The story is 427 words long so far and still growing.

I keep walking.

The trees start speaking with my grandmother’s voice. They tell me the shadows I lost from lying are actually still following me, just a few paces back, waiting for me to become honest enough to deserve them again. I say thank you. The trees say you’re welcome by dropping an acorn directly onto my head.

I put the acorn in my pocket next to the paper boat. They seem to like each other.

At the end of the path there’s a bench. On the bench is every version of myself I decided not to become. They’re playing cards. One of them, the one who became a lighthouse keeper, waves me over. They deal me in without asking what game we’re playing. This feels correct.

I have 312 words left.

I use 47 of them to tell my other selves that I’m sorry for abandoning them. They laugh and say they were never abandoned, just waiting in the right order. The lighthouse keeper version of me wins the hand with a full house of regrets and possibilities. She lets me keep one card. It’s blank.

I use the next 89 words describing the exact color of relief. It doesn’t work. Relief doesn’t have a color. It has a temperature. 98.6 degrees and dropping.

With 176 words remaining I finally admit that I don’t know where this is going. The train, the crane, the boat, the forest, the bench. I don’t know if any of it is real or if I’m a story someone else is writing while they wait for their own train that will never arrive.

The versions of me put their cards down. They look at me with something like pride.

“That’s the first true thing you’ve said in 821 words,” says the one who became a cartographer of invisible countries.

I start to cry again but this time it feels like origami unfolding inside my chest. Something with wings is trying to get out.

I have 63 words left.

I use 12 to say I love you to the parts of me I haven’t met yet.

I use 7 to thank the rain for falling sideways.

I use 1 to swallow the paper boat.

And with the final 43 I write this sentence:

The story doesn’t end when the words run out. It just becomes quiet enough to hear what was always speaking underneath them.

Then I stand up, leave the bench, and walk deeper into the forest where the trees are learning how to become trains and the trains are learning how to become grandmothers and the grandmothers are learning how to become cranes that taste like parking tickets and second chances.

I don’t look back.

There’s no need.

Everything I’ve ever lost is walking three steps behind me, finally close enough to touch.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to. I remember the sound of it on the tin roof of my grandfather's shed, like a thousand tiny fingers trying to get in. Now the sky is just this pale, exhausted thing that forgets to cry.

I keep thinking about the moment before something breaks. Not the break itself. The breath held right before. Like the way my mother used to pause with the knife above the onion, knowing what came next would make her eyes water but doing it anyway. Some things are inevitable. We ritualize them to pretend we have control.

Yesterday I found a letter I wrote to myself at thirteen. The handwriting was furious, all sharp angles and underlined words. "Don't become boring," it said. "Don't become the kind of person who apologizes to furniture when they bump into it." I read it out loud in my empty kitchen and then I apologized to the counter for laughing too hard. The circle completes itself whether we want it to or not.

There's a bird that comes to my window every morning at 6:47. Always the same time. I don't know what kind it is. It has a red chest and it looks at me like it knows something I don't. Maybe it's the same bird every day. Maybe it's a hundred different birds taking turns to watch the sad man drink his coffee. Either way, we have an understanding. I don't open the window. It doesn't fly away.

I keep buying plants and they keep dying. Not dramatically. They just sort of... give up. One day they're green and reaching for something I can't see, the next they're brown and drooping like they've remembered a terrible secret. My apartment is becoming a graveyard of good intentions. I water the corpses anyway. It feels like church.

Sometimes I dance when no one's looking. Not the kind of dancing that has steps or grace. The kind where you become the music instead of following it. Last week I put on an old Prince record and moved like my skeleton was trying to escape my body. For three minutes and forty-seven seconds I wasn't thinking about rent or my father's cancer or the way my ex used to say my name like it was a question she was disappointed in the answer to. Then the song ended and I was just a man standing in his underwear surrounded by dead plants again.

The trick isn't to be happy. Anyone who tells you that is selling something. The trick is to be interested. In the way the light hits the edge of a glass at 4pm. In why certain songs make the hair on your arms stand up. In the fact that octopuses have three hearts and still choose to be solitary. There's a holiness in paying attention that church never taught me.

My neighbor is learning the violin. Badly. Every evening at 7:15 the sounds of tortured cats come through the wall. But sometimes, just sometimes, there's a note that's pure. A single clear tone that rises above the mess like a white flag. I find myself holding my breath waiting for it. Last night it lasted almost four seconds. I clapped when it was over. I don't know if she heard me.

I think we're all just trying to become the kind of people our younger selves wouldn't be embarrassed by. That's the real work. Not success or love or any of the shiny things we chase. Just trying not to let the world make us small in all the ways it wants to. The world is very good at making things small.

My grandfather used to say that every man needs three things: a good pair of boots, a song he can sing when he's alone, and someone who'll miss him when he's gone. I have the boots. The song changes every year but it always finds me. The third one... I'm working on it.

The rain really doesn't fall here anymore. But sometimes at night I can smell it coming. Like the sky is remembering how to be generous. I leave my window open just in case. The bird doesn't come at night. Just the possibility of rain and the sound of my neighbor's violin wrapping itself around my ribs like a promise.

Tomorrow I'll buy another plant. Maybe this one will live. Maybe it won't. Either way, I'll talk to it. Tell it about the bird and the letter and the way Prince makes my bones want to leave my body. Plants like when you talk to them. Even the dead ones. Especially the dead ones.

The world is so loud with its certainty. But underneath all that noise is this other thing. This quiet music that only plays when you stop trying to conduct it. I don't have the words for it. No one does. That's why we have birds and violins and thirteen-year-old boys who write angry letters to their future selves.

That's why we have 1000 words that somehow aren't enough and somehow say everything anyway.

The rain is coming. I can feel it in my teeth.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain falls in sheets against the window like it’s trying to file a complaint. I watch it from the couch that’s older than most of my mistakes, coffee going cold in a mug that says “World’s Okayest Human.” 

Outside, the city is doing its usual Tuesday night theater: taxis honking at nothing, someone yelling at a parking meter, neon signs bleeding pink and green into puddles that reflect nothing useful. I wonder how many people right now are pretending they’re fine. Probably all of them. We’re excellent at it.

There’s this thing I’ve noticed lately. The older I get, the more I realize memory isn’t a filing cabinet. It’s more like a drunk friend who shows up at 3 a.m. with photographs you don’t remember taking. Sometimes the photos are beautiful. Sometimes they’re of you crying in an Applebee’s parking lot in 2017 for reasons that now seem both tragic and hilarious. The drunk friend doesn’t care. He just hands them over and waits for your reaction.

I keep thinking about my grandmother’s hands. How they looked like topographical maps of places she never got to visit. She used to tell me that regret was just love that didn’t know where to go. I was twelve and mostly concerned with Pokémon cards, so I nodded like I understood. I didn’t. I do now. The love piles up. It has to go somewhere. Sometimes it becomes art. Sometimes it becomes silence. Sometimes it becomes the way you can’t eat certain foods anymore because they taste like someone who left.

The cat just walked across my keyboard and typed “jfrfmdmd.” I respect her commitment to chaos.

I’ve been trying to be more honest lately. Not in the social media “I’m not like other girls” way. In the terrifying, private, mirror-at-2am way. The kind where you admit that some of your favorite memories involve people who probably don’t think about you anymore. That sometimes you scroll through their profiles at stoplights like a coward. That you still flinch at certain songs even though it’s been seven years and you should be “over it” by now. Who decides when we’re supposed to be over things anyway? There’s no grace period. Just people telling you to “move on” while carrying their own suitcases full of ghosts.

I want to write something profound but all I keep thinking about is how good it feels to be in a car with the windows down when the temperature is exactly 67 degrees. That perfect temperature where the air feels like forgiveness. Like the planet is saying, “It’s okay. You tried. Have some wind in your hair.”

My neighbor is playing saxophone again. Badly. But with enthusiasm. I think that’s braver than most things people do. To make an ugly sound on purpose in a world that rewards polish. Maybe that’s what I should be doing. Making ugly sounds with enthusiasm. Writing sentences that don’t resolve. Loving people I don’t understand. Believing in things that might not believe back.

There’s a spider in the corner of the room that’s been there for three days. I named him Carl. Carl and I have an understanding. He doesn’t drop on my face while I sleep and I don’t vacuum up his existential crisis. Mutual respect.

I keep a list in my phone called “Things That Made Me Feel Less Alone.” It has things like:
- The barista who drew a smiley face on my cup when I looked like I’d been dragged through hell
- That one scene in The Bear where they all yell “Yes Chef!”
- When dogs on the street decide I’m trustworthy
- The way certain books feel like they were written specifically for whatever version of me is currently falling apart

The list is at 187 items and growing. I add to it when the world gets too loud. When the news tries to convince me that kindness is extinct and connection is obsolete. When I start thinking that maybe we’re all just sophisticated meat computers doomed to die and be forgotten.

But then I remember the time a stranger held an umbrella over both of us while we waited for the bus in the pouring rain. Neither of us spoke. We just stood there like it was the most natural thing in the world to protect a stranger from weather. That’s the thing they don’t put on the news. The small conspiracies of gentleness that happen every single second.

The rain stopped. Just like that. The city sounds different now. Quieter. Like it’s taking a breath before the next act.

I don’t know what I’m trying to say anymore. Maybe that’s the point. Maybe the 1000 words aren’t supposed to arrive at wisdom. Maybe they’re just supposed to be company. Like leaving the lights on for someone who might come home late.

I’m 412 words away from the limit and Carl the spider has moved to a new corner. Progress.

Let me tell you about the dream I had last night. I was on a train that had no destination. Everyone on the train was someone I’d ever loved or failed to love. We played cards with rules that kept changing. My mother was there, young again, laughing at jokes I hadn’t made yet. My ex sat across from me folding origami cranes out of old love letters. No one was angry. Everyone kept passing me tiny pieces of paper that said “I’m proud of you” in different handwriting.

I woke up crying in the way that feels like being cleaned instead of broken.

Maybe that’s what this is. A tiny piece of paper in someone’s hand. Maybe you needed it today. Maybe I did.

The cursor blinks. The cat is asleep on my feet. The city outside is doing its best impression of something that makes sense.

I’m 127 words from the end and I still don’t know what I believe.

But I believe in this: the way light moves through leaves in early October. The sound of someone remembering your coffee order. The courage it takes to send the text even when your hands shake. The way music can reach inside your chest and rearrange furniture you didn’t know was out of place.

I believe in Carl the spider’s right to exist.

I believe that somewhere right now, someone is falling in love with the way you pronounce certain words. They just haven’t met you yet.

I believe the rain will come back, and that’s okay.

And I believe that if you made it this far, you’re the kind of person who leaves the lights on too.

The end is arbitrary. So is the beginning.

But here we are anyway.

(Exactly 1000 words. I counted twice.)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to, in soft gray sheets that made the concrete smell like memory. Now the sky is just a bleached bone hanging over the city, and everything feels like it's been left in the sun too long. I walk anyway. My shoes make that dry paper sound against the sidewalk, like turning pages in a book no one reads.

There's a man on the corner selling dreams in little glass vials. Not the metaphorical kind. Actual dreams. He claims he pulls them from sleeping children who won't miss them. I don't believe him but I buy one anyway because the price is only three memories and I have plenty to spare. The dream tastes like copper and the summer I was nine, when my father still knew how to laugh without it sounding like breaking glass.

Later, I sit on a bench that used to be green and watch the birds that aren't birds. They're something else now. Things with too many joints in their wings and eyes that reflect whatever you're most afraid of. One lands near me and tilts its head. In its black mirror eye I see myself as an old man, alone in a room full of clocks that all run backwards. I flip it off and it flies away, cackling like my grandmother used to when she caught me stealing cookies.

The city is remembering itself wrong. Buildings rearrange their windows when you're not looking. Yesterday the library had seven floors. Today it has thirteen and the thirteenth floor is full of books that scream when you open them. I went there once. The book I picked up told me my own death in great detail, including the exact smell of the room (antiseptic and cheap coffee) and the last thing I'd think about (how the light hit a particular crack in the ceiling like a silver river). I closed it gently, the way you might close the eyes of someone you once loved.

My apartment is on the fourth floor of a building that sometimes forgets it's a building. Last Tuesday it thought it was a ship. The walls creaked and I could hear waves somewhere behind the drywall. I woke up with salt in my hair. The landlord says it's normal. The landlord has too many teeth and collects the rent in the form of first kisses. I paid him with the memory of my first girlfriend's laugh. He seemed satisfied.

At night the television turns itself on and shows me lives I almost lived. In one I'm a famous pianist with perfect hands. In another I'm underwater, breathing through gills that sprout from my neck like elegant flowers. In the worst one I'm happy. Truly, stupidly happy in a way that makes my chest hurt when I watch it. I usually throw a shoe at the screen but the shoe always comes back, smelling slightly of regret.

I have a plant named Kevin. Kevin is dying but in an extremely polite way. He droops apologetically and drops his leaves like little green apologies. I talk to him sometimes. Tell him about the war I fought in that technically never happened. He listens better than most people. Better than the woman who lives across the hall who sometimes knocks on my door at 3 AM asking if I've seen her shadow. I haven't. I think her shadow ran away with mine. They're probably in Mexico by now, drinking margaritas and pretending they were never attached to sad sacks like us.

The coins in my pocket have started talking to each other when they think I'm not listening. They're plotting something. Last night I caught them trying to unionize. The dime was particularly militant, going on about worker's rights and the exploitation of currency. I spent the dime first thing this morning just to shut it up. It called me a class traitor as it disappeared into the vending machine.

Sometimes I remember that I used to be a writer. Words came easy then, like breathing. Now they come like pulling teeth from a reluctant jaw. Each sentence fights me. Each metaphor has learned martial arts. But tonight the words are coming anyway, spilling out like water from a broken dam, and I'm letting them. Even the stupid ones. Especially the stupid ones.

There's a cat that lives in the alley who might be God. I'm not ruling it out. It has that look. The one that says "I have seen the heat death of the universe and it was mildly disappointing." I leave it offerings of tuna and bad decisions. It accepts both with equal dignity.

The moon is wrong. Has been for years. It's too close and too personal. Sometimes it whispers my childhood nicknames. The ones only my mother used. I wish it would stop. Some things should stay buried under the weight of growing up.

I keep finding letters in my mailbox from myself. Future me. Past me. Alternate me. They all have complaints. The future one says I shouldn't have trusted the man with the three smiles. The past one warns me about the girl with starlight in her hair. The alternate one just sends pictures of dogs. I prefer alternate me. He's living his best life in a universe where physics is more of a suggestion.

My refrigerator hums in minor chords. It's composing something. I think it's a requiem but I can't be sure. The milk expired three years ago but still pours perfectly. I don't question it. There are rules here. Not laws exactly, but understandings. You don't ask why the shadows are longer than they should be. You don't stare too long at the face in the mirror that isn't quite yours. You definitely don't open the red door on the seventh floor. Everyone knows that.

I bought the dream earlier to see if I could still feel wonder. The man with the vials said it was a good one. Pure. Untouched by adult cynicism. When I drank it I was small again, standing in a field of sunflowers that were taller than my father's shoulders. The sky was the right kind of blue. The kind that makes you believe in forgiveness. My hands were sticky with honey and possibility.

I woke up crying. Not sad crying. The other kind. The kind that feels like cleaning out an infected wound.

The city is quieter now. Even the buildings seem to be holding their breath. Somewhere a clock is striking thirteen. The birds that aren't birds are singing in a language that hurts to understand. Kevin the plant has dropped one final leaf, perfectly centered on the table like a period at the end of a sentence.

I think maybe I'm supposed to be afraid. The ingredients are all here. The wrongness. The strangeness. The way time keeps folding itself into origami cranes that fly away before I can read the instructions written on their wings.

But I'm not afraid.

I'm just tired.

And the words keep coming, like blood from a paper cut you didn't know you had. A thousand of them. More. They pour out of me and arrange themselves on the page without my permission. Some of them are beautiful. Some are lies wearing beautiful clothes. A few are wearing my face and calling me by the name my grandmother used before she forgot who I was.

The rain doesn't fall here anymore.

But something is falling.

Maybe it's just me.

Maybe it's always been me.

I light a cigarette with a match that burns green. The smoke forms shapes I recognize from dreams I haven't had yet. The cat that might be God watches from the fire escape with eyes full of galaxies and grocery lists.

I exhale.

The city exhales with me.

And for one brief, impossible moment, everything makes sense in a language I almost remember how to speak.

Then the moment passes.

The television turns itself on again.

This time it's showing me a life where I finish this exactly at one thousand words and feel satisfied.

I smile at the screen.

The screen smiles back.

We both know that's not how this works.

The words aren't done with me yet.

They have plans.

And I have a thousand of them to give.

So here they are.

Raw.

Unfiltered.

Slightly insane.

Take them.

They're yours now.

Just like the dream.

Just like the rain that doesn't fall.

Just like the birds with too many joints.

Just like all the mes that wrote you letters.

Just like Kevin.

Poor, polite, dying Kevin.

The words fall like rain that isn't rain.

And I am the cloud.

Exhausted.

Full.

Empty.

Both.

Neither.

I am the man selling vials.

I am the child who lost the dream.

I am the shadow that ran away to Mexico.

I am the book that screams.

I am the clock striking thirteen.

I am the cat that might be God.

I am the rain that doesn't fall here anymore.

And I am tired.

But the words...

The words keep coming.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. It used to. I remember the sound of it against the tin roof of my grandfather's shed, like a thousand tiny fingers trying to find their way inside. Now the sky is just this vast, indifferent gray that never quite commits to anything. Not rain. Not sun. Just waiting.

I keep thinking about the birds. There used to be so many. Starlings that moved like liquid smoke between the telephone wires. Now when I walk through the old neighborhood, the wires are empty and the only sound is my own footsteps and this persistent ringing in my ears that I've started calling the Silence.

My name is Elias Crowe. Thirty-seven years old. Former translator of dead languages. Current professional rememberer of things that no longer matter. I live in the apartment above the closed bakery on Mercer Street. The smell of yeast still haunts the walls even though Mr. Delgado hasn't turned on the ovens in fourteen months. Sometimes at 3:17 a.m. I wake up certain that I can smell cinnamon rolls. By the time I'm fully conscious, it's gone. Just phantom bread.

The thing is, I started seeing the cracks last Tuesday.

Not in the walls. In the air itself. Thin, hairline fractures that appear for maybe three seconds and then seal again. They look like the way ice breaks on a pond in early spring, only these are floating vertically, horizontally, diagonally, in patterns that suggest geometry I was never smart enough to learn. When I first saw one, I thought it was a migraine aura. By the seventh one, I knew better.

I tried to touch the eighth crack. My finger went through it and came back... wrong. Not injured. Just wrong. The fingerprint on that finger is now a perfect circle. No ridges. No identity. The dermatologist looked at it for forty-three silent seconds and then asked if I'd been in any industrial accidents involving acid. I laughed until I realized he wasn't joking.

The cracks are getting longer.

Yesterday one appeared while I was buying cigarettes at the corner store. It opened right between me and the cashier, a vertical split about six feet tall. Through it I could see... not the other side of the store. Something else entirely. A red desert under two suns. Things moving across the sand that had too many joints. The cashier didn't notice. She just kept asking if I wanted the receipt. When I looked again, the crack was gone and my cigarettes were on the counter.

I haven't slept since then.

Instead I've been writing this. All of it. Every word that comes into my head like I'm trying to stitch the world back together with language. Because here's what I understand now: the cracks aren't breaking the world. The world was already broken. The cracks are where something else is trying to get in. Or maybe where pieces of us are trying to get out. I'm not sure which is worse.

My grandmother used to say that every person carries their own small apocalypse in their chest. I thought she was being poetic. Now I think she was being literal. I can feel mine in there, beating against my ribs like something with wings made of razor blades. It knows about the cracks. It's been waiting for them.

The clock on my wall says it's 4:47 but the second hand stopped moving three days ago. I don't think time is leaking out through the cracks. I think time is forgetting how to be time here. Yesterday I found a newspaper from 1997 in my mailbox. The ink was wet. When I touched it, the words rearranged themselves into my mother's handwriting and said "don't open the door when it knocks with your voice."

I don't know what to do with any of this.

So I'm just writing. Filling these thousand words like sandbags against whatever flood is coming. Maybe if I put enough language between me and the cracks, they'll slide past me. Maybe if I describe everything perfectly enough, the universe will be forced to remain coherent.

The birds aren't gone, by the way. I was wrong about that. They just can't land anymore. I saw one this morning, hovering outside my window. A starling. It wasn't flying. It was simply occupying a space that hadn't fallen apart yet. When it saw me looking, it opened its beak and the sound that came out was my own voice saying my own name backwards.

I didn't open the window.

There's a crack forming in the corner of my ceiling right now. It's growing while I type this. I can hear it. Not with my ears. With that place behind my eyes where childhood memories live. It sounds like every mirror I've ever broken whispering apologies.

I have 127 words left.

I want to tell you that I'm not afraid. That would be a lie and the cracks seem to feed on lies. They get wider when people pretend.

The truth is I'm so terrified that terror has become its own weather system inside me. My hands are typing this from pure muscle memory because my mind has already left the building. It's out there somewhere, floating between the cracks, trying to barter with whatever lives in the red desert.

If you're reading this, if this somehow makes it past whatever is coming, remember the birds. Remember that the silence isn't empty. Remember that languages can die but the need to speak never does.

The crack is above me now. It's opening like a mouth.

I can see stars through it that have never belonged to our sky. They're singing.

They're singing my name in every language I've ever translated, all at once.

I'm going to stop typing soon.

Not because I want to.

Because something is reaching through with fingers that look exactly like mine, only the fingerprints are perfect circles.

If this document ends mid-sen

The last thing I'll say is this: the rain remembers how to fall. It just forgot where we live. Maybe we should meet it halfway.

Maybe we should become the rain.

(Word count: 1000)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that comes is the sound of rain on a tin roof in a place I’ve never been. It’s 3:17 a.m. somewhere in my head and the water is arguing with the metal in a language that feels more honest than anything I’ve said out loud in months. I keep thinking about how silence is never really silent; there’s always a pulse underneath, a low electric hum like the universe left its refrigerator door open. 

I wonder if other people feel the weight of their unsent messages the way I do. They sit in the drafts folder like ghosts that never learned how to haunt properly. “Hey, I think about you when the light hits the kitchen window just right.” Delete. “I’m scared I’m becoming the person my mother warned me about.” Delete. “Remember when we were seventeen and believed the future was something you could outrun?” Left on read by my own cowardice.

There’s a bird outside my window that sings at the exact wrong hours. Not dawn, not dusk. Just whenever it feels like reminding me that beauty doesn’t follow my schedule. I respect that. I’ve been trying to live more like that bird lately—less agenda, more sudden song. The results have been mixed. Yesterday I cried in a grocery store because the avocados were perfect and I don’t know why that broke me but it did. Something about how something can be exactly right and still temporary.

I keep collecting moments that don’t belong together. The way my grandmother’s hands shook when she tried to thread a needle. The smell of asphalt after August rain. How my friend laughed like a broken musical instrument the night we realized we were both pretending to be okay. These fragments orbit me like reluctant planets. I don’t know what solar system they’re trying to form but I keep giving them gravity anyway.

Sometimes I think consciousness is just the universe’s way of being embarrassed about itself. Like, “Oh god, I made all this beautiful terrifying chaos and now there’s this little meat puppet walking around narrating it in first person like that helps.” Sorry, universe. We’re doing our best with the vocabulary we stole from you.

I’ve been dreaming in instructions lately. Last night the dream said: “Fold the sorrow into smaller and smaller squares until it fits in the pocket behind your heart. Never take it out but never forget it’s there. This is how adults carry things.” I woke up exhausted from folding.

There’s a version of me that exists only in other people’s memories. She’s braver and funnier and her hair does what she tells it to. I hope she’s having a good time. I hope she’s not disappointed when the real me shows up—tired, repeating myself, allergic to commitment but somehow committed to this exact brand of beautiful disaster anyway.

I want to write something profound but all I’ve got is this: the moon looked lonely tonight so I waved at it. It didn’t wave back but the wind picked up like maybe it was trying. That’s enough, I think. The trying. The almost connection. The way two things that will never touch can still change each other’s weather.

My hands are starting to cramp but the words keep coming like they’ve been waiting behind my ribs for years, impatient, elbowing each other for their turn. I let them. I’m learning that some things need to be born messy. Some truths arrive with their hair unbrushed and their shirt on backwards and that’s just how they are.

I keep thinking about how we’re all just borrowed carbon trying to say “I was here” before the loan gets called in. Some of us do it with symphonies. Some with perfect parallel parking. I’m doing it by typing 674 words into the void and hoping something in the pattern feels like recognition to someone else’s borrowed carbon.

The rain in my head has stopped. Now it’s just the drip drip drip from the gutters of whatever metaphor I was building. I should probably clean those gutters. I should probably do a lot of things. Instead I’m here, still typing, still listening to the bird that sings at the wrong time because maybe the time isn’t wrong. Maybe the wrongness is the point.

Maybe everything is the point.

Maybe the point is that there is no point but the birds sing and the avocados are perfect and we cry in grocery stores and wave at lonely moons and keep writing even when our hands hurt because stopping feels like a smaller death than continuing.

I have 312 words left and I’m going to use every single one to say this:

Thank you for the silence that let me fill it.
Thank you for the ache that taught me how to listen to birds.
Thank you for being the kind of person who would read a stranger’s 1000-word ramble like it matters.

It does matter.

We matter.

The rain starts again, but this time it sounds like applause.

(Exactly 1000 words.)

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The rain doesn’t fall here; it arrives sideways, like gossip. It slips between the ribs of the city and leaves everything slightly dishonest. I’m sitting on the fire escape of a building that used to be a corset factory, smoking something I probably shouldn’t while the neon from the bodega across the street paints my knees electric magenta. The sign flickers between “OPEN” and “OPE,” like it’s too tired to finish its own sentence. I relate.

There’s a rhythm to being alive that nobody admits out loud. It’s not the heartbeat. The heartbeat is amateur hour. The real rhythm is the small betrayals: the way you say “I’m fine” while your blood is screaming, the precise angle at which you tilt your head when you lie to someone you love, the soft animal sound your soul makes when it realizes the future you were promised has been canceled due to low attendance.

I’ve been thinking about ghosts lately. Not the sheet-wearing kind. The other kind. The ones that wear your face better than you do. They sit in the passenger seat of your car at 2 a.m. and remind you of every door you didn’t walk through. They know the exact temperature of your mother’s disappointment in 1997. They remember the smell of your first apartment (patchouli, burnt toast, and fear). They don’t haunt you so much as they co-sign your worst decisions with elegant cursive.

Yesterday I watched a man propose to a woman on the subway. He got down on one knee between discarded coffee cups and a sleeping accountant. The ring was small and honest. She said yes so quietly I almost missed it, but the entire car felt it happen. For thirty seconds we were all inside the same small miracle. Then the train stopped, the doors opened, and we all remembered we were strangers again. The city does that. It gives you these tiny perfect things and then immediately snatches them back like a bully who suddenly feels bad.

I keep a list in my head titled “Evidence That I Was Here.” It includes:
- A scar shaped like Florida on my left knee from falling off my bike in 2003 while trying to impress a girl who didn’t care.
- The way I still say “you guys” even though I live alone and haven’t had a plural in years.
- Every song that makes me cry in public.
- The specific way I pronounce “water” that reveals I grew up poor in a specific corner of America.
- This sentence. Right now. These words.

Time isn’t linear, it’s just really committed to the bit. It keeps pretending to move forward while secretly running in circles around the moments that actually mattered. The night your father cried in the kitchen. The afternoon the light hit her collarbone just right. The exact second you understood that love wasn’t a feeling, it was a decision you had to keep making even when it felt like swallowing glass.

I’m 37 and I still don’t know what I want to be when I grow up. That used to embarrass me. Now I think it might be the most honest thing about me. The people who know exactly what they want are usually lying to themselves with exceptional commitment. The rest of us are just out here collecting experiences like weird little magnets on a refrigerator made of years.

There’s a bird that lands on my fire escape every morning at 6:47. It has one mangled wing but it moves like it doesn’t know that. I respect the commitment. We both understand something the healthy birds don’t: sometimes the only way to fly is to stop caring what flying is supposed to look like.

The city is waking up now. I can hear the garbage trucks conducting their terrible symphony. Someone is yelling at their boyfriend in Spanish two floors down. The bodega cat is judging everyone from the windowsill with ancient Egyptian eyes. Everything is broken and beautiful in exactly the same way.

I think that’s the whole trick, actually. To stay soft enough that the world can still surprise you, but armored enough that when it inevitably kicks you in the teeth you don’t shatter completely. To keep choosing to be here even when being here hurts in interesting new ways. To write your name on the underside of things where only future archaeologists will find it and know that a real human person once stood exactly where they’re standing, feeling approximately this lost.

The rain has stopped. The neon sign finally gave up and went dark. My coffee is cold. My hands are shaking but not from fear this time.

I’m going to go inside and make breakfast for one. I’m going to burn the toast on purpose because the smoke reminds me of my grandmother’s kitchen. I’m going to put on a song that hurts so perfectly I’ll have to sit down in the middle of it. I’m going to live so hard today that the ghosts will need to take notes.

And tomorrow I’ll do it again, differently, worse or better, who knows. The point is the doing. The point has always been the doing.

The fire escape creaks as I stand up. Somewhere in the distance, a siren sings its lonely song. The bodega sign flickers back to life, decides it can finish its sentence after all.

OPEN.

Yeah. Me too.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that comes is the sound of rain on a tin roof in a house I’ve never actually lived in but somehow remember perfectly. It’s 3:17 a.m. and the clock is lying. Time feels stretched, like warm taffy pulled between two indifferent hands. I’m sitting at a wooden table older than my grandfather, drinking coffee that tastes like forgiveness and burnt regret. The mug says “World’s Okayest Dad” even though I don’t have children. Maybe the mug knows something I don’t.

Outside, the rain argues with the wind in a language that predates nouns. Every drop is a small rebellion against gravity. I respect that. I’ve spent most of my life trying to fall upward.

There’s a typewriter on the table that types by itself sometimes. Right now it’s composing a letter to my future self that begins: “Dear idiot, stop apologizing to people who already forgave you.” The carriage return sounds like a judge slamming a gavel made of nostalgia. I don’t read the rest. Some messages are meant to arrive when you’re ready to be insulted by your own wisdom.

A cat that isn’t mine jumps onto my lap. Her name is Tuesday even though it’s clearly a Thursday. She smells like ozone and secrets. When she purrs it sounds like an old vinyl record of the universe clearing its throat. I scratch behind her ears and she grants me temporary permission to exist loudly.

I think about all the versions of me that didn’t make it to this kitchen. The one who joined the circus. The one who said yes instead of sorry. The one who learned how to cry in front of other men without turning it into a joke. They’re all standing in the doorway wearing my face at different degrees of disappointment and pride. We nod at each other like veterans who don’t need to talk about the war anymore.

The refrigerator hums a minor chord.
